---
title: "3.3.1 Primary memory"
subtitle: "ROM · Hardware P122-P123"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.76em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.32em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)

## ROM: Read-Only Memory

### Permanent, non-volatile storage

<span class="term">ROM</span> (只读存储器) is a type of primary memory with permanent, unchangeable contents:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">Non-volatile memory</span> — contents are not lost when power is switched off
- <span class="mark">Permanent memory</span> — data cannot be altered or changed by the user or computer
- <span class="mark">Read-only</span> — data can be read but never written to
- Contents cannot be changed or written to by the user, the computer or any application/program
- Part of primary memory, directly accessible by the CPU
:::

## ROM: Purpose

### Bootstrap and startup instructions

<span class="term">ROM</span> stores essential data needed when the computer first boots up:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">BIOS</span> (Basic Input/Output System) — firmware that initializes hardware
- Bootstrap/startup routines that begin the loading process
- <span class="mark">Self-test procedures</span> to check hardware before loading the operating system
- Core system settings needed before any secondary storage can be accessed
:::

The CPU executes these routines automatically when power is first switched on.

## ROM: Characteristics

### Compared to RAM

<span class="term">ROM</span> differs fundamentally from <span class="term">RAM</span>:

::: {.tight-list}
- ROM cannot be increased in size (unlike RAM, which can be upgraded)
- ROM contents are determined before installation (unlike RAM, which stores variable data)
- ROM is used for <span class="mark">permanent system data</span>; RAM is temporary working storage
:::

## Table: RAM vs ROM

### Primary memory comparison

::: {.clean-table}
| Characteristic | RAM | ROM |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Temporary memory | Permanent memory |
| Volatility | Volatile | Non-volatile |
| Read/Write capability | Both read and write | Read-only |
| Data persistence | Lost at power-off | Retained always |
| Purpose | Current applications, data, OS in use | BIOS, startup routines, firmware |
| Size upgrade | Can be increased | Fixed size |
:::

## Example: Remote-Controlled Toy Car

### RAM and ROM working together

A remote-controlled toy car contains both RAM and ROM chips:

**ROM stores:**

::: {.tight-list}
- Factory settings such as remote control <span class="mark">frequencies</span>
- <span class="mark">Start-up routines</span> when the toy car is first switched on
- Set routines: how buttons control turning left, acceleration, stopping, etc.
:::

**RAM stores:**

::: {.tight-list}
- User-programmed <span class="mark">new instructions</span> and custom routines
- Data/instructions received from the remote control unit during use
:::

This shows how ROM provides permanent, factory-defined behaviour, while RAM allows the user to customise operation.

## Activity 3.6

### Practice: RAM and ROM in everyday devices

Describe how ROM and RAM chips could be used in the following devices:

::: {.tight-list}
1. A <span class="term">microwave oven</span> — which settings are permanent (ROM) and which are user-defined (RAM)?
2. A <span class="term">refrigerator</span> — how would ROM and RAM support temperature control?
3. A <span class="term">remote-controlled model aeroplane</span> controlled by a hand-held device — what goes in ROM vs RAM?
:::

Think about: factory settings vs user preferences, start-up routines vs real-time remote data.

## Classroom Check

### Know the role of ROM

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- that <span class="term">ROM</span> is <span class="mark">non-volatile</span> — data persists when power is off
- that ROM contents <span class="mark">cannot be altered</span> — it is read-only
- that ROM stores <span class="mark">BIOS and startup routines</span> needed before loading the OS
- that ROM is part of <span class="term">primary memory</span> and directly addressable by the CPU
- the key contrast: ROM is permanent and read-only; RAM is temporary and read-write
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Secondary Storage Devices

The next decks cover secondary (offline) storage devices: hard disk drives (HDD), solid state drives (SSD), optical media, and virtual memory.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)
